tldr: Camunda orchestrates BPMN workflows; Azure OpenAI Service is a hosted LLM inference platform. This is not a like-for-like swap — it only makes sense when workflow logic is being replaced by AI calls or by Azure-native services.
why_switch: Replace deterministic workflow steps with LLM-powered automation
Standardize on Azure as the single cloud and identity boundary
Cut the cost and ops burden of running a workflow engine
Move toward AI-assisted decisioning instead of BPMN/DMN tables
Consolidate procurement under an existing Azure agreement
what_to_export: Worker code and external service contracts
Historical process instance and audit data
export_method: BPMN and DMN files are portable XML — pull them from source control or Camunda Modeler as the primary artifact. Generic steps: 1) collect every BPMN/DMN and form file per project; 2) capture process variables, message names and worker contracts so they can be reframed as prompts or API calls; 3) export historical process instances via the history API for audit retention; 4) document each external task worker and the system it integrates with; 5) record identity mappings, connector credentials, dashboards and alerts. Because BPMN is an open standard, lock-in is low at the model layer, but Camunda-specific connectors, Tasklist UI and engine extensions will not transfer. Confirm exact export endpoints in the current Camunda documentation before cutover.
import_method: Azure OpenAI Service does not orchestrate workflows. Treat the migration as a redesign: 1) decide which Camunda steps become LLM calls (e.g. classification, drafting, extraction) and which need a dedicated orchestrator (Azure Logic Apps, Durable Functions, Temporal); 2) provision an Azure OpenAI resource, deploy the required model(s), and capture the endpoint and credentials (API key or Microsoft Entra ID); 3) install the appropriate SDK (e.g. @azure/openai or the OpenAI SDK pointed at the Azure base URL); 4) author and version prompts that replace BPMN logic, including evaluation tests; 5) re-implement orchestration in your chosen engine and call Azure OpenAI from worker steps; 6) validate quotas, region availability, content filters and observability. Verify current model SKUs, pricing and regions in the Azure documentation.

concept_mapping.note: Azure OpenAI alone does not execute workflows

concept_mapping.target: Azure resource API key or Entra ID identity
Custom UI or separate human-in-the-loop tool

gotchas: Azure OpenAI is not an orchestrator — without a workflow engine, retries, timers and compensation must be re-implemented elsewhere.
LLM outputs are non-deterministic; deterministic Camunda decisions need careful prompt design, structured outputs and evaluations.
Azure OpenAI access, model availability and quotas vary by region and may require approval — plan capacity early.
Content filtering can block legitimate inputs; build fallbacks for safety-rejected prompts.
Audit and compliance posture changes from an on-prem/cloud workflow engine to a managed AI service — review with security/legal.
Cost shifts from infrastructure plus licensing to per-token billing; forecast usage before scaling rollout.

email.subject: 14-day plan: Camunda wind-down + Azure OpenAI rollout
email.body:
Hi team,

Over the next 14 days we'll begin migrating off Camunda toward an Azure-first stack. Azure OpenAI Service replaces specific decision and content-generation steps, but it does not execute workflows on its own — we will pair it with an Azure-native orchestrator (Logic Apps or Durable Functions, decision pending). Plan:

1) Days 1-4: export every BPMN/DMN model, form and worker contract; archive historical process data for audit.
2) Days 5-9: provision the Azure OpenAI resource, deploy required models, and prototype the prompts that will replace selected BPMN/DMN steps. Build evaluations so we can compare against Camunda outputs.
3) Days 10-13: re-implement orchestration for the residual workflow logic and wire workers to call Azure OpenAI. Validate quotas, content filters and observability.
4) Day 14: dual-run critical processes, then freeze writes to Camunda once parity is confirmed.

Please flag any process that relies on Camunda-specific features (timers, compensation, Tasklist) so we can plan replacements before they bite us in production.

Thanks,
Your customer
